title: Paper 1 section B -writing a response
content:
Revision note for CIE English Lit Paper 1 section B.
Including terms and answering strategies.

Section B
preparing response
read questions:
Choose 1 question from 2 question choices

question 1

giving a passage from a text


question 2

no passage, whole text



time management



time spend
task




10 min
- decide question- highlight keywords on the question- annotate keywords and phrases- make brief plan of answering structure


30 min
- follow the plan and annotations- write response


5 min
- re-read and check answers



responses tips

highlight keywords in the questions
use quotations to support the idea, themselves should not be a point of view
respond to every section of the poem
last stanza:

where poet emphasises their message or attitude


give reasons for the explanation and annotations

because, as, since


link to the questions
embed the quotations into the sentence

this is shown by quote …


avoid narratives
include annotation of structure and form
conclusion of response:

personal response on the poem and questions
give new point of views



analyze prose
terms

foreshadowing

a warning or a prediction of a future event in the story
Or employ dramatic irony

readers are aware of what is coming
characters are not




flashbacks

a scene set in a time earlier than the main story
be used to convey extra information about plot or character


soliloquies

a speech which a character makes to themselves
showing a character’s inner thoughts


dialogue

conversation between two or more characters



perspectives

first-person narrator

story is told from the perspective of a character
identified by the use of “I”
make readers feel sympathy for the character
Or exposing the character as an unreliable narrator
dramatic irony

the readers know something the narrator does not know




third-person narrator

story is told from the perspective that sits outside of the world of the story
omniscient narrator:

the narrator knows everything about the characters and events





writing response
introduction

use the wording of the question to develop an introduction in your own words

main paragraphs

start with a topic sentence
use quotations and references to prove the point
explain how the evidence proves the point

question 1 strategies (passage)

detailed analysis of the passage
link to the question
comment on how the passage relates to the whole text

question 2 strategies (whole text)

discuss the structure and perspectives of the novel, linking to the question
zoom in on particular parts of the prose which are directly relevant to the question

conclusion / summary
summarize findings and response
give new point of views(no repetition of the idea)

title: The nature and variety of organisms
content:
Revision note for Edexcel IGCSE Biology.
Discussing kingdoms of organisms and characteristics of them.

Characteristics of living organisms:

movement

action by an organism causing a change of position or place
movement from place to place: locomotion
plants cannot move from place to place but change their orientation.


respiration

Chemical reaction in all organisms
Energy is released from glucose as ATP
presence of oxygen: aerobic respiration
absence of oxygen: anaerobic respiration
releasing carbon dioxide & water
$ glucose + oxygen ; -! >  carbon dioxide + water $


sensitivity

detect and respond to stimuli in the surroundings
increase the chances of survival
animals:

nervous system:

receptors
neurones
effectors
use electrical impulses


endocrine system

hormones
use chemical messengers
travel in the blood




plants:

controlled by chemicals
much slower
geotropism:

gravity
e.g. root - positively geotropism


phototropism:

light
e.g. leaf - positively phototropism






control

homeostasis
control internal environment in order to keep conditions within required limits
human:

body optimum temperature: 37°C
sweating
vasodilation
glucoregulation(blood glucose level)
osmoregulation(water level)


plants:

transpiration

water evaporates from stomata to loss heat
water is taken up from soil through roots






growth

permanent increase in size
animals

zygote -> adult
change in proportion or shape


plants

grow larger in the whole life
new shoots, leaves, branches




reproduction:

production of more of the same kind of organism
survival of species
sexual reproduction:

gametes fuse together

in humans: sperm + egg
in plants: pollen grains + ovule




asexual reproduction:

e.g. mitosis
only one parent -> clone
DNA of offspring is identical to parental DNA
plants + single-celled organisms(bacteria, amoeba)
in plants: tubers, buddings, runners




excretion

removal of toxic materials and substances from organism
metabolic reaction: chemical reactions that take place inside living cells
some of metabolic reaction waste is toxic
animal excretion waste:

carbon dioxide - respiration
water - respiration and others
urea(contain nitrogen) - break down of protein


plants excretion waste:

oxygen - photosynthesis
carbon dioxide - respiration
water - respiration and other




nutrition

obtain food for energy.
Plants(autotrophic): photosynthesis.
animals(heterotrophic): digest food from a range of different sources.


-Autotroph make their own food
-Heterotroph get food from a range of food sources






-Eukaryotic organisms:
Basics

five kingdoms of eukaryotes:

animals
plants
fungi
protoctists
Bacteria


features:

multicellular or unicellular
contain a nucleus with distinct membranes in each cell


standard animal cells:




organelle
function




nucleus
-contain genetic material(DNA)  -controls the activities of the cell(by DNA)


cytoplasm
-gel-like substances -consist of water and dissolved solutes-support internal cell structures-site of many chemical reactions (e.g. anaerobic respiration)-contains enzymes which control these reactions


cell membrane
-separating the inside of cells from outside-controls enter and leave of substances to the cell


ribosome
-found in the cytoplasm -site of protein synthesis


mitochondria
-site of aerobic respiration-release energy to fuel cellular process-cells with higher rate of metabolism reactions have higher number of mitochondria




plant cells’ specific organelles:




organelle
function




cell wall
-made by cellulose (glucose polymer)-extra support to the cell; defining shape


chloroplast
-contain chlorophyll(green) to absorb light energy-contain enzymes needed for photosynthesis


permanent vacuole
-contain cell sap - solution of sugar and salts (juice) -store certain materials-support the shape of the cell



Animals

multicellular
cells contain nuclei with distinct membranes
no cellulose cell walls
no chloroplast(photosynthesis)
heterotrophic
usually have nervous coordination
can move from place to place
store carbohydrates as glycogen

Plants

multicellular
cells contain nuclei with distinct membranes
cells contain cell walls (made by cellulose)
contain chloroplasts (photosynthesis)
autotrophic (feed by photosynthesis)
store carbohydrates as starch or sucrose
no nervous coordination

Fungi

usually multicellular; some are unicellular(e.g.yeast)

multicellular fungi are made up of hyphae containing many nuclei
are organised into a network - mycelium


have nucleus with distinct membrane
no chloroplast(no photosynthesis) - heterotrophic
contain cell walls (made by chitin)
store carbohydrates as glycogen
saprotrophic nutrition

feed by secreting extracellular digestive enzymes(outside of mycelium)
absorb the digested molecules



Protoctists

unicellular or multicellular
have nucleus with distinct membrane
some have chloroplast - autotrophic
some have cell walls
store carbohydrates as glycogen
no nervous coordination


-Prokaryotic organisms:
features:

always single-celled
do not contain a nucleus
have nuclear material in cytoplasm in prokaryotic cells
smaller than eukaryotic cells

Bacteria

unicellular microscopic organisms
no nucleus (but contain circular DNA)
contain cell walls
no mitochondria
no chloroplast - heterotrophic and autotrophic
store carbohydrates as glycogen or PHB

title: Paper 1 section A -writing a response
content:
Revision note for CIE English Lit Paper 1 section A.
Including structure and language techniques and answering tips.

Section A
answering tips
time management



time spend
task




10 min
- decide question- highlight keywords on the question- re-read the poem- annotate- make brief plan of answering structure


30 min
- follow the plan and annotations- write response


5 min
- re-read and check answers



Key words

explore: write in detail
how: examine techniques the writer has used to achieve a specific effect
vividly / convey: produce powerful feelings and strong clear images in mind
how far / to what extent: an element of the text allowing to make a counter-argument
portray: explore how writer describe or bring a character or a theme to life in text.
memorable: how writer presents a character of event in a way that stands out  / worth remembering

responses tips

highlight keywords in the questions
use quotations to support the idea, themselves should not be a point of view
respond to every section of the poem
last stanza:

where poet emphasises their message or attitude


give reasons for the explanation and annotations

because, as, since


link to the questions
embed the quotations into the sentence

this is shown by quote …


avoid narratives
include annotation of structure and form
conclusion of response:

personal response on the poem and questions
give new point of views



structure and forms of poems
Forms / genre

sonnet

14 lines
usually theme of love
petrarchan sonnet(Italian)

ABBA, ABBA, CDE, CDE rhyme scheme


shakespearean sonnet(English)

ABAB, CDCD, EFEF, GG rhyme scheme




epic

ancient
lengthy narrative poem
typically heroic deeds


free verse

a lack of rules
can rhyme or not
about any themes


villanelle

19 lines
five stanza of 3 lines
final stanza of 4 lines
only 2 rhyming sounds(ABA)


ode

originally performed with musical instruments
praise a person, event or thing
short in length


ballad

telling dramatic or emotional story
ABAB or ABCB rhyme scheme




poets may alter the rhyme scheme, subverting a traditional form to convey an idea

structure



techniques
explanation




stanza length
changes on the length of lines


repetition
repetition of words, phrase or ideas


caesura
using punctuations at the mid of a line


enjambment
a lack of punctuation at the end of a line


shift
change in ideas and perspectives


contrast
difference between two things


pace
the feeling of speed in the writing


temporal references
references to time


dialogue
conversations and speech



Language
Terms of language

comparative adjectives

used to compare difference between 2 nouns
e.g. larger, smaller, faster


superlatives

used to express something of the highest quality of its kind
e.g. fairest, prettiest, funest


emotive language

words and phrases that are used to evoke strong emotional responses in the reader


colloquial language

informal, everyday language


imperatives

verb forms or sentences that are used to give commands
e.g. “Fill your belly”, “Day and night make merry”



language techniques

simile

directly compare two different things
use “like”,“as”


metaphor

describe one thing in terms of another
indirectly compare


imagery

engage the readers senses by using vivid and detailed language to create mental image


alliteration

repetition of consonant sounds
e.g. beech buggy


personification

give objects human characteristics or movements


onomatopoeia

words that imitate or mimic the sounds


repetition

repeat of words, phrases, sounds or structure to create emphasis


juxtaposition

contrast
place two contrasting things side by side to highlight the differences


assonance

repetition of vowel sounds
sounds similar
e.g. moonlit pool, cock of the walk


rule of three

list of 3 things or ideas
e.g. freedom, equality and justice


rhetorical questions

a question asked for effect with no answer expected


emotive language

words or phrases that can bring an emotional response


hyperbole

exaggeration for effect

title: Circulatory system/cardiovascular system
content:
Revision note for Edexcel IGCSE Biology.
discussing circulatory system, including blood, vessels and heart.

Blood
plasma

pale yellow liquid
carrying everything that needs transporting around hunan body:

blood cells:

red blood cell
white blood cell
platelets
digested food products - from gut to all body cells
carbon dioxide
urea - from liver to the kidneys (be removed in the urine)
hormones
heat energy





platelets

small fragments of cells
help blood clotting

stop body losing too much blood
prevent microorganisms from entering the wound
In a clot:

platelets are held together by a protein called fibrin
other proteins call clotting factors are needed to work properly in this process





red blood cell

carry and transport oxygen from the lung to all the cells in the body
adaptational features:

small with a biconcave shape

larger surface area for absorbing and releasing oxygen.


contain haemoglobin

contain iron - gives blood its colour
reacts with oxygen to become oxyhaemoglobin
reverse reaction happens to release oxygen to the cells


no nucleus

more space for more haemoglobin
carry more oxygen





White blood cells

most important part of immune system
phagocytes

ingest pathogens


lymphocytes

produce antibodies




more white blood cells’ information in immune system part.


Vessels
Arteries branch into capillaries, and capillaries eventually join upp to form veins
Arteries

features:

carry the blood away from the heart
carry oxygenated blood (except the pulmonary artery)
transport blood in high pressure
transport blood in fast speed
thick muscular walls

contain elastic fibres


narrow lumen


adaptational features:

thick muscular walls containing elastic fibres

withstand high pressure of blood
maintain high pressure of blood


narrow lumen

maintain high pressure




aorta - largest artery in the body

Veins

features:

carry blood toward the heat
carry deoxygenated blood (except pulmonary vein)
transport blood in low pressure
transport blood in slow speed
thin walls
large lumen
contain valves


adaptational features:

large lumen:

reduce resistance to blood flow under low pressure


valves:

prevent the backflow of blood




vena cava - largest vein in the body

Capillaries

features:

very tiny
carry both oxygenated and deoxygenated blood
transport blood in low pressure
transport blood in slow speed
thin walls

one cell thick


carry blood very close to every cell

exchange substances with cells


supply food and oxygen
take away wastes (e.g. CO_2)


adaptational features:

one-cell-thick walls

decrease diffusion distance
increase rate of diffusion


permeable walls

substances are easy to diffuse in and out






Heart

cardiac: heart

structure
left atrium

connected with pulmonary vein
receive oxygenated blood from lungs

left ventricle

connected to aorta
pumped out oxygenated blood around the body
adaptational features:

thick wall - more muscles

higher pressure
pump blood around the whole body - long distance





right atrium

connected to vana cava
receive deoxygenated blood from the body

right ventricle

connected to pulmonary artery
pumped out deoxygenated blood to the lungs

Valves

preventing blood flowing backwards
in main vessels:

semi-lunar valve

pulmonary artery
aorta




inside heart:

bicuspid valve == left atrium ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ left ventricle
tricuspid valve == right atrium ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ right ventricle




coronary heart disease (CHD)

coronary arteries:

blood vessels supplying blood(oxygen,glucose) to the heart muscle (cardiac muscle)


coronary arteries get blocked by layers of fatty material building up

formed from cholesterol
two source of cholesterol

dietary cholesterol (from eaten animal products)
cholesterol synthesised by the liver




angina

partial blockage
restricted blood flow to the cardiac muscle cells
severe chest pains


heart attack

complete blockage
lack of oxygen
heart cells can not respire aerobically



risk factors:

diet high in saturated fat

fatty deposits inside arteries


smoking

increases blood pressure
cause damage to the inside of the coronary arteries
fatty deposits inside arteries
carbon monoxide will decrease the oxygen capacity of red blood cells





circulatory system
lesser circuit (circuit through lung)

deoxygenated blood flow through vena cava into the right atrium
right atrium contracts and blood flows through tricuspid valve into right ventricle
right ventricle contracts and blood flows through the semilunar valve in the pulmonary artery
blood flows through the pulmonary artery into the lungs, through the capillaries around alveoli
gas exchange - deoxygenated blood ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ oxygenated blood
oxygenated blood flows back through teh pulmonary vein into the left atrium


vena cava ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ right atrium ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ right ventricle ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ pulmonary artery ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ lungs ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ pulmonary vein ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ left atrium ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ left ventricle
valves: tricuspid valve / semilunar valve

greater circuit (circuit through the whole body)

left atrium ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ left ventricle through bicuspid valve
left ventricle ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ aorta, through the semi-lunar valve
aorta ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ rest of body

aorta ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ brain ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ brain vein ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ vena cava
aorta ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ hepatic artery ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ liver ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ hepatic vein ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ vena cava
aorta ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ mesenteric artery ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ gut ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ hepatic portal vein ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ liver ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ hepatic vein ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ vena cava
aorta ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ renal artery ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ kidney ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ renal vein ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ vena cava






Organ
vessels in
vessels out




Heart
Vena cava
pulmonary vein


Lung
pulmonary artery
pulmonary vein


kidney
renal artery
renal vain


gut
mesenteric artery
hepatic portal vein




heart rate
Heart rate :
measured by counting the number of times a heart beats in a minute (bpm)
Pacemaker:

a group of cells in teh right atrium
coordinate the contraction of heart muscle

by sending electrical impulses which initiate a contraction in the cardiac muscle


control the natural resting heart rate


Heart when exercising

heart pumps blood to cells:

supply oxygen and glucose
remove respiratory waste


factors:

exercise
adrenaline level


during exercise:

heart rate increase ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ more frequent
volume of blood increase ⟶\longrightarrow⟶ larger volume


after exercise:

heart rate remain high for a period of time

to break down lactic acid from anaerobic respiration (oxygen required)






moderate exercise -mainly aerobic respiration
intense exercise -mainly anaerobic respiration

title: reasoning exercise vocabulary
content:
vocabulary list for new words I found in English reasoning exercise papers.

Week 1
Daffodil(n.):
a tall yellow spring shaped like a trumpet.
Related vocabulary: Wales, daffodil lament, blooming.
Obtuse(adj.):
1.slow or unwilling to understand sth.
2.Obtuse angle: over-180-degree angles
Related vocabulary: smart, right angle, acute angle
Saucepan(n.):
a deep round metal pot with a lid and one long handle or two short handles.(used for cook things over heat)
Related vocabulary: stewpot, pan.
Pastry(n.):
1.A mixture of flour, fat and water or milk that is rolled out flat and baked as a base or covering for pies.
2.A small cake made using pastry
Related vocabulary: cake, cupcake.
Pyramid(n.):
1.A large building with a square or triangular base and a sharp top in ancient Egypt.
2.A solid shape with a square or triangular base and sloping side that meet in a point at the top.
3.An object or a pile of things that has the shape of a pyramid.
Related vocabulary: pyramidal, Pharaoh
Pause(n./v)
1.to stop talking or doing something before continuing.
2.A period of time during which sb stops talking or stops what they are doing.
3.A control that allows you to stop a tape recorder, CD, etc;
Related vocabulary: continue, break.
Lukewarm(adj)
1.Slightly warm;
2.Not interested or enthusiastic
Related vocabulary: uninterested, chilly, lukewarmness.
Idle(adj/v)
1.Not working hard
2.(of machines, factories,etc)Not in use
3.Without work
4.Without particular purpose or effect; useless
5.Not spent doing work or sth
Related vocabulary: lazy, unemployed, idleness
Intend(v)
1.To have a plan, result or purpose in your mind when you do sth
2.To plan that sth should have a particular meaning.
Related vocabulary: mean, intention.
Refuse(v,n)
1.To say that you will not do sth that sb has asked you to do
2.Waste materials that has been thrown away
Related vocabulary: turn down, deny, garbage
Nasty(adj)
1.Very bad or unpleasant
2.dangerous or serious
3.Offensive, in bad taste
Related vocabulary: mean, unkind, pleasant
Concord(n)
1.peace and agreement
2.The fact of having the same number, gender or person
Related vocabulary: harmony, discord, agreement
Tranquillity(n)
A state of peace and quiet
Related vocabulary: quiet, repose
Serenity(n)
The state of being calm, peaceful, and untroubled
Related vocabulary: serenities.
Maim(v)
To injure sb seriously, causing permanent damage to their body.
Related vocabulary: disable, maimed
Cripple(v/n)
1.To damage sb’s body so that they are no longer able to walk or move normally
2.To serious damage or harm ab/sth
3.A person who is unable to walk or move normally because of a disease or injury.
Related vocabulary: break, lameness
Heal(v)
1.To become healthy again; to make sth healthy again
2.To cure sb who is ill; to make sb feel happy again
3.To put an end to sth or make sth easier to bear;
Related vocabulary: healing(adj), healer.
Espy(v)
To see sb/sth suddenly
Related vocabulary: spy, spot
Blemish(n/v)
1.A mark on the skin or on an object that spoils it look less beautiful or perfect
2.To spoil sth that is beautiful or perfect in all other ways
Related vocabulary: defect, soil, mud, spot
Sting(v/n)
1.To touch your skin or make a very small hole in it so that you feel a sharp pain
2.To feel, or to make sb feel a sharp pain in a part of their body
3.To make sb feel angry or upset.
4.To charge sb more money than they expected;
5.The sharp pointed part of an insect or creature that can go into the skin leaving a small, painful wound.
6.Any sharp pain in your body or mind
7.A clever secret plan by the police to catch criminals.
8.A clever plan by criminals to cheat people out of a lot of money.
Related vocabulary: stimulus, stick, pink, bite
Chic(adj)
Very fashionable and elegant
Related vocabulary: stylishness, fashion
Dowdy(adj)
1.not attractive or fashionable
2.Dull or boring and not attractive
Related vocabulary: ancient, callet.
Toss(v)
1.To throw sth lightly or carelessly
2.To move your head suddenly upward, especially to show that you are annoyed or impatient.
3.To move or make sb/sth move from side to side or up and down
4.To shake or turn food in order to cover it with oil, butter, etc
5.To throw a coin in the air in order to decide sth, especially by guessing which side is facing upward when it lands. (toss sb for sth)
6.An act of throwing a coin in the air in order to decide sth
7.An act of move your head suddenly upward, especially to show that you are annoyed or impatient.
8.An act of throwing sth, especially in a competition or game
Related vocabulary: cast, wap, wallow
Fling(v,n)
1.to throw sb/sth somewhere with force, especially because you are angry.
2.To move yourself or part of your body suddenly and with a lot of force
3.To say sth to sb in an aggressive way
4.A short period of enjoyment when you do not allow yourself to worry or think seriously about anything
Related vocabulary: put, cast, hurtle
Bashful(adj)
Shy and easily embarrassed
Related vocabulary: shy, blate
Wary(adj)
Careful when dealing with sb/sth because you think that there may be a danger or problem
Related vocabulary: cautious, prudent
Recorder(n)
1.A machine for recording sound or pictures or both.
2.A musical instrument in the shape of a pipe that you blow into, with holes that you cover with your fingers.
3.A person who keeps a record of events or facts.
Related vocabulary: tape recorder
Promptly(adv)
1.Without delay
2.Exactly at the correct time or at the time mentioned
3.Immediately(always used before the verb)
Related vocabulary: punctually, quickly, rapidly.
Bail(n/v)
1.money that sb agrees to pay if a person accused of a crime does not appear at their trial. When bail has been arranged, the accused person is allowed to go free until the trial.
2.To release sb on bail
3.To leave a place, especially quickly.
4.To approach sb and talk to them, often when they do not want this.
Week 2
Squally(adj)
Involving sudden, violent and strong winds(weather)
Related vocabulary: showers, squall
Sultry(adj)
1.Very hot and uncomfortable
2.Sexually attractive(a woman or her appearance)
Related vocabulary: muggy, sexy
Blizzard(n)
1.A snowstorm with very strong winds
2.A large quantity of things that may seem to be attracting you.
Palm(n/v)
1.The inner surface of the hand between the wrist and the fingers
2.A straight tree with a mass of long leaves at the top, growing in the tropical countries.
3.To hide a coin, card, etc. In your hand, especially when performing a trick.
Related vocabulary: vola
Decade(n)
1.A period of ten years, especially a period such as 1990-1999
Related vocabulary: decennium, dix
Sleet(n/v)
1.A mixture of rain and snow.
2.When it is sleeting, a mixture of rain and snow is falling from the sky.
Related vocabulary: verglas, sleety
Ballad(n)
1.A song or poem that tells a story
2.A slow popular song about love
Related vocabulary: derry, volkslied, balladeer
Elect(v,sdj)
1.To choose sb to do a particular job by voting for them
2.To choose to do sth
3.Used after nouns show that sb has been chosen for a job, but is not yet doing that job.(e.g.the president elect)
4.People who have been chosen to be saved from punishment after death.
Related vocabulary: remarkable, choose
Penitent(adj/ n)
1.Feeling or showing that you are sorry for having done sth wrong
2.A person who shows that they are sorry for doing sth wrong, especially a religious person who wants God to forgive
Related vocabulary: confessional, confessor, penitence
Bill(n/v)
1.A piece of paper that shows how much you owe sb for goods or services
2.A piece of paper that shows how much you have to pay for the food and drinks that you had in a restaurant.
3.To send sb a bill for sth
4.To advertise or describe sb/sth in a particular way
5.To advertise that sb/sth will do sth.
Related vocabulary: statement, invoice
Adore(v)
1.To love sb very much
2.To like sth very much
Related vocabulary: love, sweetheart.
Sage(n/adj )
1.A plant with flat light green leaves that have a strong smell and are used in cooking as a herb
2.A very wise person
3.Wise, especially because you have a lot of experience
Related vocabulary: herb, philosopher,
Ponder(v)
To think about sth carefully for a period of time
Related vocabulary: consider.
Mend(v/n)
1.To repair sth that has been damaged or broken so that it can be used again
2.To find a solution to a problem or disagreement
3.To improve in health after being ill/sick
4.To heal(of a broken bone)
Related vocabulary: recover, improve, modify
Rear(n/adj/v)
1.The back part of sth
2.The part of the body that you sit on
3.At or near the back of sth
4.To care for young children or animals until they are fully grown
5.To breed or keep animals or birds, for example on a farm
6.To raise itself on its back legs, with the front legs in the air
7.To seem to lean over you, especially in a threatening way(sth large)
Related vocabulary: raise, back, aback, set
Week 3
Sombre(adj)
1.dark in colour, dull
2.Sad and serious
Related vocabulary: drab, melancholy, upset, dark
Daybreak(n)
The time of day when light first appears
Related vocabulary: dawn
Govern( adj )
1.To legally control a country or its people and be responsible for introducing new laws, organizing public services, etc
2.To control or influence sb/sth or how sth happens, function, etc
3.If a word governs another word or phrase, it affects how that word or phrase is formed or used.
Related vocabulary: manage, possess, conduct, regulate.
Timid(adj)
Shy and nervous; not brave
Related vocabulary: modest, harehearted
Brisk(adj)
1.Quick, busy
2.Practical and confident; showing a desire to get things done quickly
3.Cold but pleasantly fresh
Related vocabulary: alive, acute, keen, lively, activated
Bold(adj)
1.Brave and confident, not afraid to say what you feel or to take risks
2.That can be easily seen; having a strong clear appearance
Related vocabulary: rapid , stouthearted
Operate(v)
1.To work in a particular way
2.To be used or working; to use sth or make it work
3.to cut open sb’s body in order to remove a part that has a disease or to repair a part that is damaged
4.To be involved in military activities in a place
Related vocabulary: act, handle, operation
Counterfeit(adj/v)
1.Made to look exactly like sth in order to trick people into thinking that they are getting the real thing
2.To make an exact copy of sth in order to trick people into thinking that it is the real thing

2.To become or make sb completely involved in sth

Contort(v)
To become twisted or make sth twisted out of its natural or normal shape

Panorama(n)

2.A description, study or set of pictures that presents all the different aspects or stages of a particular subject, event, etc

Conceit(n)

2.An artistic effect or device, especially one that is very clever or tries to be very clever but does not succeed

Week 4
Overcast(adj ):
Covered with clouds;dull
Related vocabulary: cloudy, dull,shadow, shield
Kindle(v)
1.To start burning; to make a fire start burning
2.To make sth such as an interest, emotion, etc. Start to grow in sb; to start to be felt by sb

Potent(adj)
1.Having a strong effect on your body or mind
2.Powerful

Scrub(v/n)

2.To cancel sth that you have arranged to do
3.An act of scrubbing
4.Small bushes and trees
5.The special clothes worn by surgeons when they are doing medical operations
Related vocabulary: small, scouring, depurate
Anticipate(v)
1.To expect sth
2.To see what might happen in the future and take action to prepare for it
3.To think with pleasure and excitement about sth that is going to happen
4.To do sth before it an be done by sb else
Related vocabulary: hope, expect
Gossip(n/v)
1.Informal talk or stories about other people’s private lives, that may be unkind or not true
2.A conversation about other people
3.A person who enjoys talking about other people’s private lives
Related vocabulary: essay, quidnunc
Impede(v)
To delay or stop the process of sth
Related vocabulary: hinder, hamper
Agile(adj)
1.Able to move quickly and easily
2.Able to think quickly and in an intelligent way
Related vocabulary: nimble, fly, sharp , keen
Enclosure(n)
1.A piece of land that is surrounded by a fence or wall and is used for a particular purpose
2.The act of placing a fence or wall around a piece of land
3.Something that is placed in an envelope with a letter
Related vocabulary: attachment, pen
Week 5
Apparel(u):
1.Clothing, when it is being sold in shops/stores
2.Clothes, particularly those worn on a formal occasion
Related vocabulary: clothing, garment, array, wear.
Bleach(v/n):

to make sth white or pale by a chemical process or by the effect of light from the sun; to become white or pale in this way
3.A chemical that is used to make sth become white or pale and as a disinfectant.
Related vocabulary: snow, whiten, decolorant, whitener.

Emerge(v)
1.To come out of a dark, confined or hidden place
2.To become known
3.To start to exist; to appear or become known
4.To survive a difficult situation or experience
Related vocabulary: appear.
Blend(v/n)
1.To mix two or more substances together
2.To form a mixture with sth
3.To combine with sth in an attractive or effective way; to combine sth in this way
4.To produce sth by mixing different types together
5.A mixture of different types of the same thing
6.A pleasant or useful combination of different things(a blend of youth and experience 年轻而且有经验)
Related vocabulary: compound, mixture, sort
Week 6
Solitary(adj/n)
1.Done alone; without other people
2.Enjoying being alone; frequently spending time alone
3.Alone with no other people or things around
4.Only one(in negative sentences and questions)
Related vocabulary: alone, neighborless, hermit, anchorite, solitarily, solitude, solitariness
Plaice 比目鱼
Hake  鳕鱼
Sole 可食用比目鱼
Lamb(n/v)
1.A young sheep
2.Meat from a young sheep
3.Used to describe or address sb with affection or pity
4.To give birth to a lamb
Related vocabulary: kidlet, yeanling, lamblike
Veal(n)
Meat from a calf(a young cow)
Related vocabulary: calf, runt
Foe(n)
An enemy
Related vocabulary: enemy, anti, opponent
Adversary(n)
A person that sb is opposed to and competing with in and argument or a battle
Related vocabulary: equal, opponent, comparative, rival, match
Novel(n/adj)
1.A story long enough to fill a complete book,in which the characters and events are usually imaginary
2.Different from anything known before; new, interesting and often seeming slightly strange
Related vocabulary: patent, exquisite, story, fiction
burst in on sb/sth:
to interrupt sb/sth by entering a place suddenly and noisily
Related: barge
Pallid(adj)
1.Pale, especially because of illness
2.Not strong or bright, and therefore not attractive
Related vocabulary: gray, dirty, pale, wan
Faint(adj/v/n)
1.That cannot be clearly seen, heard or smelt
2.Very small; possible but unlikely
3.Not enthusiastic
4.Feeling weak and tired and likely to become unconscious

6.The state of becoming unconscious

Conceal(v)
To hide sb/sth
Related vocabulary: lock, pocket, to hide
Week 7
Lettuce(n)
A plant with large green leaves that are eaten raw, especially in salad. There are many types of lettuce
Related vocabulary: william, banknote
Effortless(adj)
Needing little or no effort, so that it seems easy
Related vocabulary: easy, light, easiest
Diverse(adj)
Very different from each other and of various kinds
Different, unlike, varying, another, dissimilar
Curtail(v)
To limit sth or make it last for a shorter time
Related vocabulary: crop, to cut back
Stretch(v/n)
1.To make sth longer, wider or looser, for example by pulling it; to become longer, etc. In this way
2.To become bigger or longer when you pull it and return to its original shape when you stop
3.To pull sth so that it is smooth and tight
4.To put you arms or legs out straight and contract you muscles
5.To put out an arm or a leg in order to reach sth

7.To continue over a period of time
8.To be enough to buy or pay for sth
9.To make use of a lot of your money, supplies, time, etc.
10.To make use of all sb’s skill, intelligence, etc.
11.To use sth in a way that would not normally be considered fair, acceptable, etc
12.An area of land or water, especially a long one
13.A continuous period of time
14.A period of time that sb spends in prison
15.An act of stretching out your arms or legs or your body and contracting the muscles; the state of being stretched
16.The ability to be made longer or wider without breaking or tearing
17.A straight part at the end of a racing track
Related vocabulary: lengthen
Tardy(adj)
Slow to act, move or happen; late in happening or arriving
Related vocabulary: slow-motion, overdue, late for, late arrival.
Punctual(adj)
Happening or doing sth at the arranged or correct time, not late
Related vocabulary: accurate, precise, mathematical, strict, rigid
Cradle(n/v)
1.A small bed for a baby which can be pushed gently from side to side

Crib(n/v)

Hamper(n/v)

Bazaar(n)

title: Cell Structure
content:
Revision note for biology Alevel Topic 1.
explaining organelles in cells.

Cell Structure
cell wall

support & protection
prevention from bursting
material

cellulose = plant
chitin = fungi
peptidoglycan = bacteria



cell surface membrane

bilayer of phospholipid

polar hydrophilic phosphate head
non-polar hydrophobic fatty acid tail


controls substances entry / exit

nucleus

contain DNA & RNA
make ribosomes & rRNA
nuclear pore:

allow mRNA movement


chromatin:

DNA + histone proteins


nuclear envelope:

double membrane
outer connects with RER



mitochondria

aerobic respiration

ATP production

small soluble molecule
release energy by hydrolysis reaction


take place in matrix & cristae


contain DNA & 70s ribosomes
bilayer

protect the matrix (inner membrane)


matrix

interior of the mitochondria
contain enzymes in solution



plasmodesmata

pore like tubes
link adjacent plant cells
contain fine stands of cytoplasm
lined with the cell membrane
controls the movement of molecules by the shape
symplast pathway

water movement between cells via the plasmodesmata



chloroplast

bilayer
carry photosynthesis
stroma:

site of light-independent stage of photosynthesis
circular DNA
70s ribosomes
enzymes


thylakoids:

site of light-dependent reactions
large surface area



microtubules

hollow tubes made of tubulin protein
make up the cytoskeleton

support and movement of the cell



centriole

contain 9 microtubules
help chromosomes separate during cell division
production of cilia & flagella
centrosome

centrioles lie together at right angle



cilia

membrane-bound
microtubule-containing
flexible
types of cilia

primary cilia

sensory function


motile cilia

movement of organisms or substances
e.g. trachea





golgi apparatus

a stack of curved membranes
consist of a group of stacked, thin, fluid filled membranes.
near the nucleus
“post office of the cell”

Cis face: receives proteins and lipids from other organelles.
Cisternae & inner lumen: packaging the lipids and proteins

Golgi vesicles:

special membrane bound parcels where proteins and lipids are packaged.




Trans face:

release the proteins or lipids after package




make lysosomes

lysosome

round membrane bound organelles

No distinct internal structure
A type of golgi vesicle


contain lysozymes

A digestive enzyme

Digest materials in the cell


A hydrolytic enzyme

Facilitate hydrolysis reactions




cellular “digestive systems”

Contain hydrolytic enzymes
Can break down macromolecules (include: proteins, carbohydrates, lipids)


be used to digest invading cells & digest worn out cell structures

Mechanisms of defense against infections


excrete digested material

Smooth ER (smooth endoplasmic reticulum)

no ribosomes (“smooth”)
in the cytoplasm of eukaryotic cells
extends from the nuclear envelope towards the membrane
functions:

synthesis lipids

happen inside the cisternae
e.g. cholesterol, steroids hormone


detoxify toxins

in the liver


major storage site for calcium ions



Rough ER (Rough endoplasmic reticulum)

the surface is covered in ribosomes
contain stacked, fluid-filled membranes
fold and package proteins -> send them to the golgi apparatus

ribosome

small organelles

free in the cytoplasm
attached to the Rough ER


site of protein synthesis
formed from rRNA and proteins
mRNA sits between the two subunits
no membrane
composed of a small subunit & large subunit

large subunit

site of translation




type of ribosomes:

80s

60s + 40s
in eukaryotic cells


70s

50s + 30s
in prokaryotic cells
in mitochondria and chloroplasts of eukaryotic cells





vacuole

membranous sacs
store cell sap

water
nutrients
waste materials


maintain cell pressure and structure
tonoplast

single membrane of vacuole

title: Cell 2025 10
content:
Diseases are not determined by one gene
Scientists have discovered that the symptoms of many complex diseases are not caused by a single gene, but by an entire“Genetic background”. Just like the performance of a mobile phone, not only by a chip, other components are also important.


The“Pits” on the surface of skin cancer cells turn out to be staging grounds for immune killers
The study found that melanoma cells have pits on their surface where immune cells like to gather to attack the tumor. This suggests that the shape of the cancer cell’s surface also affects the immune response.


A new technique for“Reading” all RNA in tissueーー RAEFISH
Yale University has developed a microscopic technique that can locate RNA molecules of almost any gene in a tissue section. Like mapping where each inhabitant lives, it can be very helpful in understanding how genes work in the body.


How is the“Skeleton” disassembled?
German scientists have revealed how three key proteins that disassemble the cell’s internal“Cytoskeleton”(actin) work together, which is important for understanding how cells move and cancer cells spread.


There’s a whole-body pattern behind the salamander’s amazing regeneration
The Harvard team found that when a salamander loses a limb, its nervous system sends signals that put stem cells throughout its body on“Standby” for repair. This is an important clue for the study of human regenerative medicine.


How the fruit fly brain determines whether an odor is“Sweet” or“Smelly.”
The Riken team found the neural circuits in the flies’ brains that control“Like” or“Hate” odors, providing a model for understanding the relationship between emotion and smell.


Two types of“Cleaner” cells in the brain talk to each other
Research at the University of Massachusetts Medical School has found that microglia and astrocyte in the brain communicate and work together to regulate the remodeling of neural connections, which is linked to neurodevelopment and diseases such as Alzheimer’s.


Scientists have mapped proteins in the cell’s antennae
The team mapped the proteins of more than 100,000“Primary cilia”-tiny antennas on cells-and found many new proteins that could help in the study of ciliopathies, a genetic disease in children.


Chinese team reveals new evolutionary mechanism of CRISPR system
Research by the Chinese Academy of Sciences and Tsinghua University has found that some CRISPR systems-the immune systems of bacteria-have evolved through“RNA unbundling” mechanisms, which could help develop more precise gene-editing tools.

title: Transport in animals
content:
Revision note for Alevel Biology Topic 8
explaining transport in animals.


Topic 8 – Transport in Mammals
The Circulatory System
Closed Double Circulatory System


Mammals have a closed double circulatory system


The system consists of:

heart
blood
blood vessels (arteries, arterioles, capillaries, venules, veins)



Closed circulatory system

Blood is always contained within blood vessels
Allows higher pressure and faster transport

Double circulation


Blood passes through the heart twice in one complete circulation:

Pulmonary circulation
Systemic circulation



**Advantages **

High systemic blood pressure
Faster transport of oxygen and nutrients
Supports a high metabolic rate


Pulmonary and Systemic Circulation
Pulmonary circulation

Right ventricle → lungs → left atrium
Low pressure
Prevents damage to lung capillaries

Systemic circulation

Left ventricle → body tissues → right atrium
High pressure
Efficient delivery of oxygen and nutrients


Main Blood Vessels



Vessel
Function




Pulmonary artery
Carries deoxygenated blood from heart to lungs


Pulmonary vein
Carries oxygenated blood from lungs to heart


Aorta
Carries oxygenated blood from heart to body


Vena cava
Carries deoxygenated blood from body to heart




Recognition of Blood Vessels
Artery

Thick wall
Thick tunica media
Narrow lumen
Circular shape
No valves

Vein

Thin wall
Large lumen
Irregular shape
Valves present

Capillary

Wall one cell thick (endothelium only)
Very narrow lumen
Red blood cells pass in single file


Structure Related to Function
Elastic arteries

Many elastic fibres
Stretch during systole
Recoil during diastole
Maintain continuous blood flow and pressure

Muscular arteries

Thick smooth muscle layer
Vasoconstriction and vasodilation
Control blood flow to organs

Arterioles

Very muscular walls
Regulate blood distribution

Veins

Thin walls
Large lumen
Valves prevent backflow
Skeletal muscle contraction aids blood return

Capillaries

One cell thick → short diffusion distance
Permeable walls
Narrow lumen → slow blood flow
Large total surface area


Blood Cells
Red Blood Cells

Diameter ≈ 7 µm
Biconcave disc shape → increased surface area
No nucleus → more space for haemoglobin
No mitochondria → do not use oxygen transported
Flexible → pass through capillaries

Neutrophils

Lobed nucleus
Carry out phagocytosis

Lymphocytes

Large nucleus
Produce antibodies

Monocytes

Kidney-shaped nucleus
Differentiate into macrophages


Water in Transport
Properties of water

Good solvent for polar substances
High specific heat capacity

Importance

Transport of glucose, amino acids, ions
Maintains stable body temperature


Tissue Fluid
Functions

Medium for exchange between blood and cells
Supplies oxygen and nutrients
Removes carbon dioxide and waste products

Formation of Tissue Fluid

High hydrostatic pressure at arteriole end of capillary
Plasma forced out of capillary walls
Large proteins remain in capillaries
Fluid surrounds cells → tissue fluid
Lower pressure at venule end allows reabsorption
Oncotic pressure draws fluid back into capillaries


Transport of Oxygen
Haemoglobin

Conjugated protein
Quaternary structure
Four haem groups
Each haem group binds one oxygen molecule


Oxygen Dissociation Curve


S-shaped (sigmoid) curve


Shows relationship between oxygen saturation and partial pressure of oxygen


Cooperative binding:

Binding of first oxygen increases affinity for next oxygen



In lungs

High pO₂
Haemoglobin becomes saturated

In respiring tissues

Low pO₂
Oxygen is released

Importance

Steep section allows large oxygen unloading with small drop in pO₂


Bohr Shift

Increased CO₂ concentration
CO₂ lowers pH
Oxygen dissociation curve shifts to the right
Haemoglobin has lower affinity for oxygen

Importance

More oxygen released to actively respiring tissues


Transport of Carbon Dioxide
Methods of CO₂ Transport



Method
Approx. %




As hydrogencarbonate ions
~70%


As carbaminohaemoglobin
~23%


Dissolved in plasma
~7%




Role of Carbonic Anhydrase


Catalyses:
CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃


Haemoglobinic Acid

Hydrogen ions bind to haemoglobin

Carbaminohaemoglobin

Carbon dioxide binds to globin chains of haemoglobin


Chloride Shift

Hydrogencarbonate ions diffuse out of red blood cells
Chloride ions diffuse into red blood cells
Maintains electrical neutrality

Importance

Allows continued transport of CO₂ in blood


Structure of the Heart
External Structure

Pericardium
Coronary arteries supply heart muscle

Internal Structure


Four chambers:

Left atrium
Right atrium
Left ventricle
Right ventricle



Septum separates left and right sides


Valves:

Atrioventricular (AV) valves
Semilunar valves




Wall Thickness


Atria: thin walls → receive blood


Ventricles: thick walls → pump blood


Left ventricle:

Thickest wall
Pumps blood at high pressure to whole body




Cardiac Cycle
Definitions

Systole: contraction
Diastole: relaxation

Stages

Atrial systole
Ventricular systole
Diastole

Valves

AV valves close → “lub”
Semilunar valves close → “dub”


Control of Heartbeat


Sinoatrial node (SAN)

Acts as pacemaker
Initiates heartbeat



Atrioventricular node (AVN)

Delays impulse
Allows ventricles to fill



Purkyne tissue

Conducts impulse rapidly
Causes coordinated ventricular contraction

title: Markdown note 1 - CDLI evidence bank
content:
Markdown note for EPQ project No 1
notes for evidence used for CDLI refining


CDLI Evidence Bank
Purpose
This note was created during the later stage of my EPQ to organise evidence related to the Composite Driver Limitation Index (CDLI).Also, I grouped the evidence by model variable instead of recording them source by source.
CDLI formula
CDLI = 0.25T + 0.15Cv + 0.25G + 0.15V + 0.20C
T = thermal-hypohydration load
Cv = cardiovascular load
G = G-force / muscular load
V = vibration load
C = racing-specific cognitive load
Thermal-hypohydration load
Evidence used


Heat stress and hypohydration literature


Motorsport heat studies


Dehydration and cognitive performance meta-analysis


Dr Chris Tyler’s feedback


How it supports the model
This evidence supports the idea that heat and body water loss can increase physiological strain of the driver and very likely to reduce cognitive reserve.
Limitation
Direct F1 core temperature and body mass loss data are not open to the public, so this variable cannot be fully validated using official F1 race data.
Cardiovascular load
Evidence used


Real time F1 heart rate case study


Motorsport physiology literature


Physiological Strain Index concept(PSI)


How it supports the model
Heart rate can provide a measurable sign of physiological strain during racing.
Limitation
Heart rate is affected by heat energy, stress, adrenaline level, steering effort and G-force load, so it cannot be treated as a pure measurement of physical workload.
G-force / neck muscular load
Evidence used


Elite motor racing driver neck strength study


Official high-G circuit examples


Dr Chris Tyler’s feedback on neck and G-force tolerance


How it supports the model
G-force is highly specific to Formula One because drivers repeatedly experience lateral and braking loads. Neck strength are important for maintaining visual control and steering precision by stabalising head.
Limitation
There is no published academic articles showing exactly how repeated lateral G-force changes lap time or decision-making accuracy.
Vibration load
Evidence used


Whole-body vibration and driver drowsiness studies


Driving vibration studies on reaction time


F1 porpoising context


How it supports the model
Vibration may affect alertness, visual stability and control precision.
Limitation
Public F1 vibration amplitude and exposure data are limited, so this variable is included cautiously.
Racing-specific cognitive load
Evidence used


Otto Lappi’s work on racing cognition


Eye-tracking simulator studies


MRLIN mental workload source


Dr Lappi’s expert feedback


How it supports the model
Racing cognition should not be reduced to reaction time. It includes anticipation, gaze strategy, visual attention and decision-making under high competition pressure.
Limitation
There is no F1 specific evidence such as physiological academic essays showing exactly how heat, G-force or vibration change F1 drivers’ gaze behaviour during real races.
Reflection
This note helped me move from a descriptive essay to a more analytical model. It also helped me avoid double-counting. For example, if heat increases cardiovascular strain, I needed to make sure that T and Cv did not both represent the same effect too strongly. This is why the final CDLI model is presented as an exploratory comparison tool rather than a precise predictive model.

title: Markdown note 2 - excluded/weak source review
content:
Markdown note for EPQ project No 2
evaluating excluded source


Excluded / Weak Source Review
Source considered
Aston Martin F1 Team feature article: “Autopilot: 6 reasons F1 drivers are just like fighter pilots”
Why I first considered it


Its explanation of why Formula One drivers are sometimes compared with fighter pilots is very easy to understand and friendly to unspecialist.


It mentioned the terms: G-force, concentration and physical preparation in a easy way.


At the early stage of the project, it can help me think about the relationship between acceleration forces and driver performance.


Why I did not use it as main evidence


It is a team feature article, not a peer-reviewed academic source.


The comparison between F1 drivers and fighter pilots is useful as an analogy, but it can easily become exaggerated.


no detailed method for driver performance measurement or any official experimental data are suggested


It is written for a general audience, so it is not strong enough to support the scientific argument by itself.


How I used it
I used it only as background reading during the early research stage. It helped me identify G-force as an important area to investigate, but I later replaced it with stronger sources, including peer-reviewed motorsport physiology papers, aviation G-force research and expert feedback.
Decision
Use only as background / do not use as main evidence.
Reflection
This source is a good example of why source evaluation was important in my EPQ. At first, it looked useful because it explained the topic clearly. However, after developing my source evaluation table, I realised that clear explanation is not the same as strong evidence. This helped me separate accessible background sources from sources that could genuinely support my dissertation.
